description: Two leading economists argue that India's main problems lie in neglect of essential needs of the poor and women, examining how corruption, institutional failures, and the lack of social infrastructure undermine high economic growth and democratic governance.

title: Quality of Bureaucracy and Open-Economy Macro Policies
description: Examines how variation in bureaucratic quality across countries affects open-economy macroeconomic policies, specifically the imposition of capital controls and financial repression in developing economies.

title: Evolution of the World Bank's Thinking on Governance
description: Background paper for WDR 2017 tracing the evolution of the World Bank's thinking on governance across its flagship publications, from WDR 1997 on 'The State in a Changing World' through WDR 2004 on service delivery to contemporary approaches.

title: A Systematic Review of the Effects of Cash Transfer Programs on Schooling Outcomes
description: Systematic review examining both conditional and unconditional cash transfers' effects on schooling, finding that the impact depends on moderating factors including monitoring, transfer size, recipient identity, and baseline enrollment rates.

title: World Social Protection Report 2024-26: Asia and the Pacific
description: ILO report highlighting key challenges for social protection in Asia-Pacific from a life-cycle perspective, addressing COVID-19 aftermath, climate change threats, persistent informality, and demographic shifts across the region.

title: Evaluating Anti-Poverty Programs
description: Comprehensive review arguing that no single evaluation method dominates and that rigorous evaluations should be open-minded about methodology. Covers experimental and non-experimental approaches to anti-poverty program evaluation.

title: Experimentation at Scale
description: Makes the case for larger-scale randomized experiments in development economics, addressing concerns about spillovers, political economy effects, and heterogeneity that arise from small-scale RCTs.

title: Radical Decentralization: Does Community-Driven Development Work?
description: Review synthesizing RCT evidence on community-driven development programs, finding effective delivery of public goods but little evidence of lasting transformation of local institutions or empowerment of the poor.

title: Reshaping Institutions: Evidence on Aid Impacts Using a Preanalysis Plan
authors: Katherine Casey, Rachel Glennerster, Edward Miguel
description: Pioneering study using a pre-analysis plan in an RCT of a governance program in Sierra Leone, demonstrating how PAPs guard against data mining and showing divergent interpretations possible without pre-specification.

title: Randomization and Social Policy Evaluation Revisited
description: Nobel laureate Heckman revisits the case for randomized experiments in economics, identifying two historical waves of enthusiasm and demonstrating that experimental designs rest on unstated assumptions.

title: Scaling Up What Works: Experimental Evidence on External Validity in Kenyan Education
authors: Tessa Bold, Mwangi Kimenyi, Germano Mwabu, Alice Ng'ang'a, Justin Sandefur
description: Demonstrates that positive effects from NGO-led RCTs of contract teachers in Kenya disappear under government implementation, highlighting critical challenges of external validity and scaling in development experiments.

title: How Much Can We Generalize from Impact Evaluations?
description: Analyzes 635 papers on 20 types of development interventions and finds large effect heterogeneity, with government-implemented programs having smaller effects than NGO-implemented ones, raising concerns about generalizability.

title: Learning from Experiments When Context Matters
description: Demonstrates that non-experimental evidence from the relevant context can outperform experimental evidence from other contexts based on mean-squared error, highlighting the trade-off between internal and external validity.

title: Decision Theoretic Approaches to Experiment Design and External Validity
authors: Abhijit Banerjee, Sylvain Chassang, Erik Snowberg
description: Develops a decision-theoretic framework for experimental design that clarifies questions about pre-analysis plans, experimental registries, and external validity, arguing external decision-making remains inherently subjective.

title: The Short-Term Impact of Unconditional Cash Transfers to the Poor: Experimental Evidence from Kenya
description: Exemplary RCT studying GiveDirectly's unconditional cash transfers in Kenya, randomizing recipient gender, transfer timing, and magnitude. Finds significant impacts on consumption, assets, and psychological well-being.

title: Regression Discontinuity Designs: A Guide to Practice
description: Seminal practical guide to regression discontinuity designs, covering sharp and fuzzy designs, bandwidth selection, specification tests, and graphical analysis for applied researchers.

title: Regression Discontinuity Designs in Economics
description: Comprehensive user guide for regression discontinuity designs in economics, explaining when RD is valid given economic incentives, why it is quasi-experimental, and how to estimate and interpret RD results.

title: Some Practical Guidance for the Implementation of Propensity Score Matching
description: Widely cited practical guide covering all key implementation decisions for propensity score matching: estimation of propensity scores, choice of matching algorithm, common support, matching quality assessment, and sensitivity analysis.

title: What's Trending in Difference-in-Differences? A Synthesis of the Recent Econometrics Literature
authors: Jonathan Roth, Pedro H.C. Sant'Anna, Alyssa Bilinski, John Poe
description: Synthesizes the rapidly evolving difference-in-differences literature, covering staggered adoption, heterogeneous treatment effects, pre-trend testing, and best practices for practitioners.

title: Difference-in-Differences with Variation in Treatment Timing
description: Shows that the standard two-way fixed effects DD estimator is a weighted average of all possible two-group/two-period DD estimators, which can produce biased estimates with staggered treatment timing.

title: Design-based Analysis in Difference-In-Differences Settings with Staggered Adoption
description: Takes a design perspective on DiD with staggered adoption, showing properties of standard estimators under random assignment of adoption dates and proposing improved inference procedures.

title: Instrumental Variables: An Econometrician's Perspective
description: Reviews instrumental variables methods from an econometrics perspective, connecting classic supply and demand applications to modern applications in randomized experiments with noncompliance.

title: Synthetic Control Methods for Comparative Case Studies: Estimating the Effect of California's Tobacco Control Program
authors: Alberto Abadie, Alexis Diamond, Jens Hainmueller
description: Introduces the synthetic control method, which constructs a data-driven weighted combination of comparison units to estimate causal effects when only a single treated unit is available.

title: Causal Inference: The Mixtape
description: Free online textbook covering causal inference methods including DAGs, potential outcomes, matching, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables, difference-in-differences, and synthetic control with R and Stata code.

title: Beyond Baseline and Follow-Up: The Case for More T in Experiments
description: Argues that for noisy outcomes common in development economics (business profits, incomes), multiple survey rounds increase statistical power more efficiently than the standard baseline-plus-single-followup design.

title: How to Do a Good Systematic Review of Effects in International Development: A Tool Kit
authors: Hugh Waddington, Howard White, Birte Snilstveit, Jorge Garcia Hombrados, Martina Vojtkova, Philip Davies, Ami Bhavsar, John Eyers, Tracey Perez Koehlmoos, Mark Petticrew, Jeffrey C. Valentine, Peter Tugwell
description: Practical guide for conducting systematic reviews in international development, covering search strategies, critical appraisal, meta-analysis, and a theory-based approach to drawing generalizable conclusions from 3ie's experience.

title: Narrative Approaches to Systematic Review and Synthesis of Evidence for International Development Policy and Practice
authors: Birte Snilstveit, Sandy Oliver, Martina Vojtkova
description: Argues for applying systematic review methods to qualitative evidence in development, introducing narrative synthesis approaches ranging from content analysis to meta-ethnography for broader evidence questions.

title: Using Case Studies to Explore the External Validity of 'Complex' Development Interventions
description: Provides a framework for assessing external validity of complex development interventions using case studies, arguing that the default assumption about generalizability should be zero for implementation-intensive programs.

title: Integrating Quantitative and Qualitative Research: How Is It Done?
description: Content analysis of 232 mixed methods social science articles examining how quantitative and qualitative research are actually combined in practice, finding that stated rationales and actual uses often diverge.

title: Mixed Methods Research in Developing Country Contexts: Lessons From Field Research in Six Countries Across Africa and the Caribbean
description: Examines how logistical challenges and cultural contexts (race, colonial legacies, aid-dependence, gender norms, religion) in developing countries affect mixed methods design and implementation across six countries.

title: Integrating Reflexivity into Livelihoods Research
description: Proposes a methodological approach for reflexive livelihoods research based on Burawoy's extended case method and critical realism, overcoming the positivism-constructivism dualism in development studies.

title: Whose Reality Counts? Putting the First Last
description: Seminal book on participatory rural appraisal (PRA) methods arguing that development professionals must reverse their top-down approaches and recognize the knowledge and analytical capabilities of poor and marginalized communities.

title: Applying Participatory Action Research Methods in Community-Based Adaptation With Smallholders in Myanmar
authors: Julian Gonsalves, Rakhshanda Bano, Eisen Bernardo, Khoy Chhim, Felicity Chancellor, Julian Gonsalves
description: Presents experiences from a 3-year participatory action research project on community-based climate adaptation in four Climate-Smart Villages in Myanmar, demonstrating PAR methodology in development contexts.

title: Participatory Action Research: New Uses, New Contexts, New Challenges
description: Examines the research outcomes of participatory action research, discussing arguments, tensions, dilemmas, and opportunities for achieving research impact through participatory approaches.

title: A Scoping Review of Participatory Research Methods in Agroecology Studies Conducted in South Asia
description: Reviews participatory research methods used in 27 agroecology studies in South Asia, finding diverse methods applied primarily in India but noting farmers are rarely engaged as study collaborators or decision-makers.

title: Implementing Qualitative Methods in the Field
description: J-PAL resource providing an overview of commonly used qualitative tools for development research, covering integration with RCTs, hiring and training qualitative field staff, sampling, and data analysis.

title: Qualitative Approaches in Poverty Studies: A Structured Literature Review
description: Bibliometric analysis identifying qualitative techniques used in poverty research from 2015-2020, discussing advantages and disadvantages of in-depth interviews, focus groups, and participatory methods.

title: Designing Household Survey Questionnaires for Developing Countries: Lessons from 15 Years of the Living Standards Measurement Study
description: World Bank guidance on designing multi-topic household surveys for developing countries, drawing on 15 years of LSMS experience to provide detailed advice on objectives, data needs, and questionnaire design.

title: Development Research in Practice: The DIME Analytics Data Handbook
authors: Kristoffer Bjarkefur, Luiza Cardoso de Andrade, Benjamin Daniels, Maria Ruth Jones
description: Open-access handbook from the World Bank's DIME department covering modern, transparent, and ethical research practices for development data including survey design, data management, and reproducible analysis.

title: Power Calculations
description: J-PAL resource providing an intuitive discussion of statistical power calculations for randomized evaluations, including practical tips and sample code for conducting power analyses using simulation.

title: Introduction to Randomized Evaluations
description: J-PAL overview providing a non-technical introduction to randomized evaluations, covering when RCTs are useful, when they may not be the right choice, and key design considerations.

title: Promises and Perils of Pre-Analysis Plans
description: Examines the advantages and costs of pre-specifying statistical analysis plans in economics, finding that 10-20% of conventionally significant results may be misallocated due to data mining.

title: Transparency, Reproducibility, and the Credibility of Economics Research
description: Surveys evidence on publication bias, replication failures, and specification searching in economics, and discusses progress through study registration, pre-analysis plans, disclosure standards, and open data sharing.

title: Evaluating Replicability of Laboratory Experiments in Economics
authors: Colin F. Camerer, Anna Dreber, Eskil Forsell, Teck-Hua Ho, Juergen Huber, Magnus Johannesson, Michael Kirchler, Johan Almenberg, Adam Altmejd, Taizan Chan, Emma Heikensten, Felix Holzmeister, et al.
description: Replicated 18 laboratory experiments from top economics journals and found significant effects in the same direction for 61%, with replicated effect sizes averaging 66% of originals. Published in Science as a free article.

title: Research Transparency and Reproducibility
description: J-PAL resource documenting 15 years of leadership in research transparency, including the development of hypothesis registries, data sharing on Harvard Dataverse, and collaboration with BITSS and Center for Open Science.

title: Reflections on the Ethics of Social Experimentation
description: Discusses research ethics in field experiments in developing areas, highlighting limitations of standard consent procedures, risks to research partners, and the need for constructing appropriate ethics for social experiments.

title: Towards Decolonising Research Ethics: From One-off Review Boards to Decentralised North-South Partnerships in an International Development Programme
description: Argues that limiting ethics to informed consent and anonymity cannot address larger power relationships in North-South research, proposing an 8-step model towards decolonising research ethics. Open access via MDPI.

title: Fostering Research Integrity in Sub-Saharan Africa: Challenges, Opportunities, and Recommendations
description: Open access paper highlighting ethics dumping, parachute research, and questionable incentive structures in global health research, calling for equity and fairness throughout the research cycle in the Global South.

title: Women's Empowerment and Economic Development: A Feminist Critique of Storytelling Practices in 'Randomista' Economics
description: Feminist critique of RCT-based development economics arguing that the approach produces piecemeal analysis, ad hoc theory, indifference to history and context, and methodological fundamentalism that overlooks structural gender inequalities.

title: Randomized Control Trials and Qualitative Evaluations of a Multifaceted Programme for Women in Extreme Poverty: Empirical Findings and Methodological Reflections
description: Compares RCTs and qualitative assessments of BRAC's asset transfer program in West Bengal and Sindh, finding that RCTs failed to meet their own causality criteria and provided limited explanations for observed outcomes. Open access under CC BY.

title: Field Experiments and the Political Economy of Development
description: Describes the range of field experiments in political economy of development, identifying frontiers for research and discussing challenges particular to experimental work in developing country settings.

title: How to Build M&E Systems to Support Better Government
description: World Bank IEG publication providing practical guidance on building, strengthening, and institutionalizing monitoring and evaluation systems, drawing on case studies from Chile, Colombia, Australia, and Uganda.

title: The PRISMA Statement for Reporting Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses of Studies That Evaluate Health Care Interventions: Explanation and Elaboration
authors: Alessandro Liberati, Douglas G. Altman, Jennifer Tetzlaff, Cynthia Mulrow, Peter C. Gotzsche, John P.A. Ioannidis, Mike Clarke, P.J. Devereaux, Jos Kleijnen, David Moher
description: The PRISMA reporting standard for systematic reviews with a 27-item checklist and four-phase flow diagram. While originating in health, widely adopted in development research. Open access via PLOS Medicine.

title: 3ie Impact Evaluation Practice: A Guide for Grantees
description: Practical guide from 3ie for researchers conducting impact evaluations of development interventions, covering experimental and quasi-experimental designs, theory of change, and quality assurance standards.

title: Running Randomized Evaluations: A Practical Guide
description: Comprehensive practical guide from J-PAL leadership covering end-to-end design and implementation of randomized evaluations, including power calculations, randomization, survey design, and ethical considerations.

title: Pre-Analysis Plans
description: J-PAL resource on writing and implementing pre-analysis plans for randomized evaluations, drawing on Olken (2015) and Casey, Glennerster, and Miguel (2012) to provide practical guidance on reducing data mining.
